hook: You already know that a linear transformation $T: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$ can be represented by a matrix, and that matrix multiplication lets you apply $T$ to any vector. But what happens when you apply one transformation after another — say, first rotate, then reflect? And if you apply a transformation $T$, can you always "undo" it to get back to where you started? These questions lead to two of the most powerful ideas in linear algebra: composition and invertibility.
concept: Composing two linear transformations means applying them in sequence: if $S: \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}^n$ and $T: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$, their composition $T \circ S: \mathbb{R}^p \to \mathbb{R}^m$ is defined by $(T \circ S)(\mathbf{x}) = T(S(\mathbf{x}))$. In matrix terms, if $S$ has matrix $B$ and $T$ has matrix $A$, then $T \circ S$ has matrix $AB$. A transformation $T$ (with square matrix $A$) is invertible if there exists $T^{-1}$ such that $T^{-1} \circ T = T \circ T^{-1} = I$, equivalently $A^{-1}A = AA^{-1} = I$.
anatomy: In the composition $T \circ S$, the right-hand transformation $S$ is applied first and is called the inner transformation, while $T$ is applied second and is called the outer transformation — order matters because matrix multiplication is not commutative, so $AB \neq BA$ in general. For invertibility of $T$ with matrix $A$: the matrix $A$ must be square ($n \times n$), its determinant $\det(A)$ must be nonzero (the invertibility criterion), and the inverse matrix $A^{-1}$ satisfies $A^{-1}A = I_n$, where $I_n$ is the $n \times n$ identity. The transformation $T^{-1}$ is the geometric "undo" of $T$.

worked_example.description_latex: We are given two linear transformations $S$ and $T$, each from $\mathbb{R}^2$ to $\mathbb{R}^2$, with the matrices shown. Our goal is to find the matrix of the composition $T \circ S$ (apply $S$ first, then $T$) and determine whether this composition is invertible.

The matrix of $T \circ S$ is the product $AB$ where $A$ is the matrix of $T$ and $B$ is the matrix of $S$. Notice carefully that $A$ (the outer transformation) goes on the left and $B$ (the inner transformation) goes on the right — this matches the fact that $T$ acts after $S$, and matrix multiplication reads right to left.

We carry out the matrix multiplication entry by entry using the row-times-column rule. Each entry $(AB)_{ij}$ is the dot product of row $i$ of $A$ with column $j$ of $B$, giving us the matrix shown above.

To determine invertibility, we compute the determinant of the composition matrix. Because $\det(AB) = -3 \neq 0$, the composition $T \circ S$ is an invertible transformation — there exists a linear map that perfectly undoes it.

Using the $2 \times 2$ inverse formula $\frac{1}{ad-bc}\begin{pmatrix}d & -b \\ -c & a\end{pmatrix}$, we find the matrix of $(T \circ S)^{-1}$. We can verify: $(AB)(AB)^{-1} = I_2$, confirming the result. Note also the useful identity $(AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}$ — the inverses in reverse order.

partial_example.description_latex: We want to find the matrix of the composition $T \circ S$ and check whether it is invertible. Let $A$ be the matrix of $T$ and $B$ be the matrix of $S$; recall that the matrix of $T \circ S$ is the product $AB$ with $A$ on the left.
Here is the result of the matrix multiplication. Take a moment to verify one or two entries yourself before moving on — for example, confirm that the $(2,2)$ entry $(-1)(1) + (2)(3) = 5$ is correct.

Now it is your turn: compute $\det(AB)$ for the matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & 5 \end{pmatrix}$, and state whether $T \circ S$ is invertible. If it is invertible, write down the matrix $(AB)^{-1}$ using the $2 \times 2$ inverse formula.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $B = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute the matrix of $T \circ S$ where $T$ has matrix $A$ and $S$ has matrix $B$.
Let $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. Determine whether the linear transformation with matrix $A$ is invertible, and if so, find $A^{-1}$.
Let $T$ have matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$. Is $T$ invertible? Justify your answer.

Suppose $T: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ has matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and $S: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}^2$ has matrix $B = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -5 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$. Compute $T \circ S$ and $S \circ T$ and determine whether composition is commutative here.
practice_problems.answer_latex: AB = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = BA \text{; in this special case they commute because } B = A^{-1}.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A manufacturer applies two successive linear processes to a production vector $\mathbf{x}$: first a scaling $S$ with matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$, then a mixing $T$ with matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$. Find the matrix of the combined process $T \circ S$ and determine whether the combined process can be reversed (i.e., is invertible). If so, find its inverse.

common_mistakes: Students write the composition matrix as $BA$ instead of $AB$ when computing $T \circ S$ — remember that the inner transformation $S$ (applied first) corresponds to the right matrix $B$, and the outer transformation $T$ (applied second) corresponds to the left matrix $A$, so the correct product is $AB$.

Students assume that if $T$ is invertible and $S$ is invertible, then $(T \circ S)^{-1} = T^{-1} \circ S^{-1}$ — but the correct formula reverses the order: $(T \circ S)^{-1} = S^{-1} \circ T^{-1}$, equivalently $(AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}$.

Students check invertibility only by looking at whether the transformation 'looks' like it should be reversible geometrically, instead of computing the determinant. Only a nonzero determinant guarantees an inverse exists; always compute $\det(A)$ explicitly.
untested_variants: Composing three or more linear transformations (e.g., $T \circ S \circ R$) and finding the matrix $ABC$ and its inverse using $(ABC)^{-1} = C^{-1}B^{-1}A^{-1}$.

Determining invertibility for transformations between spaces of different dimensions (non-square matrices), where the notion of a full inverse does not apply and students must instead discuss left- or right-inverses.
Using the property $\det(AB) = \det(A)\det(B)$ to determine invertibility of a composition without explicitly computing the product matrix $AB$.

Finding the matrix of a composition when the transformations are defined by their geometric action (e.g., 'rotation by $90°$ followed by reflection across the $x$-axis') rather than by an explicit matrix.
